Trading TTC · crypto swing system · plain-English field guide

Annie's Trading System — the Rulebook, in Plain English

Everything from the course, rewritten for a human. What she does, when she does it, the exact rules, and the handful of things even she never spells out (those are flagged for you to answer).

On this page

  1. How to read this
  2. 1 · The market model
  3. 2 · The building blocks
  4. 3 · The 7 setups
  5. 4 · Risk rules
  6. 5 · 8 questions for you
  7. 6 · Glossary

How to read this

Annie (Trading TTC) doesn't trade random chart patterns. Her whole system rests on one idea: a big "market maker" (think of the whale who moves price) runs the same script most weeks — fake everyone out early, trend for a few days, reverse mid-week, and set one last trap on Friday. If you know the script, you can wait for the trap and take the other side. This rulebook walks you through that script, then the small "vocabulary" pieces she reads on the chart, then the seven specific trades she takes, and finally how she sizes and protects each one.

Read it top to bottom once, then use the menu to jump around. Every trading word gets a plain gloss the first time it appears, and there's a full glossary at the end. When the course genuinely never answers something, you'll see an orange "What we still don't know" box — those aren't gaps in this document, they're gaps in the course, and the 8 questions near the end have all been answered by Igor (11 Jul 2026) and folded back into the rules. Screenshots are from her own screen; click any image to open it full-size. Nothing here is invented — every number traces back to her videos or your backtest notes.

Part 1

The Market Model — her week and her day

Before any setup, she frames when she is. Two clocks matter: the trading day and the trading week. Both are anchored to New York time, and the key hour is 5:00pm New York — that's when a new "day" starts for her.

The day: four sessions in a fixed order

Each 24 hours cycles through the same four trading sessions. She expects a different behaviour from each. The whole point: the UK session builds a trend, and the US session usually fakes one way then reverses it about 1.5–2 hours after the US open.

quiet after US ▸
ranges / chops ▸
builds the trend ▸
fakes then reverses ▸
Dead Zone
after ~5pm NY (US close)
The post-US lull. Low volume, no trades. The daily reset (5pm NY) lives here.
Asia
evening NY
Price ranges sideways and sets the day's early high & low. If Asia's range is bigger than 2% on Bitcoin, she doesn't trade that day.
UK
from ~3am NY
The trend session — a clean move that runs roughly 6–8 hours. Her 3:30–3:45am "Brinks" trade lives at the Asia→UK handover.
US
from ~9:30am NY
The reversal session. Makes a false move, then reverses the UK trend ~1.5–2h after the open. Her 9:30–9:45am Brinks trade lives at the UK→US handover.

The exact clock boundaries between sessions aren't the point — the order and the character are. The two hard time-anchors she actually trades off are the two "Brinks" candles (3:45am and 9:45am NY), covered in Setup 1.

The week: one repeating script

Her week runs Sunday 5pm NY → Sunday 5pm NY (the weekend is included). Across those days the market maker tends to follow this script — and she has a name for each phase:

Sun / Mon
The fake move
Price spikes out of the weekend range to hunt stops — she calls it the FMWB ("fake move at week's beginning"). Your backtest: these peak on Monday 14 of 17 times.
Mon → Wed
The real trend
The genuine move runs in three legs (Level 1, 2, 3), with a pause called a board meeting between each.
Wed / Thu
Mid-week reversal
The week's trend often flips — usually on a news event (CPI, FOMC). She calls this the MWR.
Friday
The weekend trap
A false move at the UK open, repeated near the UK close. This is a take-profit signal, never an entry.
Fri close
Flat by the weekend
Close weekly trades by the end of Friday's UK session. Never hold over the weekend on majors.
Rule of thumb she repeats: "The market maker attempts either the high of the week or the low of the week every week." Fake-move weeks move about 4% on average; full trend weeks move about 10% — so you trade them differently (small target on a fake-move week, ride the legs on a trend week).

High & low of the week / day (her map lines)

She marks four levels and trades around them. They're the "walls" the market maker aims at:

  • HOW / LOW — High / Low Of the Week: the highest and lowest price (wick tips) since last Sunday 5pm NY.
  • HOD / LOD — High / Low Of the Day: same idea for the last 5pm→5pm day.
  • IHOD / ILOD — the inner high/low, one step inside HOD/LOD. The shaded band between the inner and outer line is an Area of Interest (AOI) — a zone she watches for reactions. Her indicator draws all of these automatically.

Part 2

The Building Blocks — the vocabulary

Every setup is built from these seven pieces. Learn them once and the setups read like sentences.

a. PVSRA candles — colour = volume

Her candles are coloured by how much volume traded, not just up/down. This is a free, public indicator called PVSRA — anyone can add it and get the exact same colours. The rule is simple arithmetic:

Green — up candle, volume ≥ 200% of the last 10 candles' average Red — down candle, ≥ 200% Blue — up, ≥ 150% Magenta — down, ≥ 150% Grey / white — normal volume

A coloured candle means big players just did something. One caution she gives: a candle can go coloured just because the candles before it were unusually quiet, so colour alone isn't a signal — it has to also stand out as a spike on the volume bars at the bottom of the screen.

b. Vectors & the "unrecovered vector"

A vector is just one of those high-volume coloured candles — a footprint of a big player. The one that matters most is the unrecovered vector: a vector that price has not traded back through yet. She treats the base of an unrecovered vector as a magnet and a target — price tends to come back and "fill" it. Her very first profit target on most trades is the origin (base) of the Level-1 vector, cross-checked against a low-volume gap ("crevice") on a volume-by-price tool.

c. SVC — the Stopping Volume Candle

The candle that says "this move is exhausted."

An SVC is a small body with a long wick and a volume spike, appearing at the end of the third leg (Level 3). It's the market maker's last shove — grabbing the final stops before reversing. Around it she draws the SVC box: the candle's full wick-to-wick range, extended to the right. That box is the "trapped-traders" zone.

The one box rule that matters: price is allowed to poke back into the SVC box (any depth) — that's fine. The box is only invalidated by a solid candle that closes all the way through the far edge (past the SVC's extreme wick). A poke that closes back inside re-confirms it.
Igor ruling · 11 Jul 2026Shape is fixed, size is subjective. The spike only needs to be a PVSRA high-volume candle (higher volume than usual); what matters is the shape — a long wick (either side) with the body smaller than the wick. Price stops there and usually reverses. On timeframes under 1H, an engulfing candle also qualifies as the stopping signal.

d. The EMA stack — five trend lines

EMA = a moving average that leans on recent prices (better for fast crypto than a plain average). She runs five at once — 10, 20, 50, 200, 800 — on every timeframe:

  • 10 & 20 (fast) — momentum. A trade usually starts when price crosses these on a second M/W peak.
  • 50 (mid) — the gatekeeper. Level 1 only counts if a volume vector's body closes across the 50.
  • 200 & 800 (slow) — the destinations. Leg 1 runs to the 200 and rejects; Leg 2's target is the 800 (or the next unrecovered vector).

Two shapes to read: when the lines fan out (spread wide apart) at Level 3, that looks like strong momentum but is actually the trap. When they flatten and bunch, that's a board meeting (a pause).

Igor ruling · 11 Jul 2026Fan-out is a judgment call, not a hard filter. There is no taught number. Treat it as an optional proxy — e.g. the 10-EMA gap to the stack ≥ 2–3× the recent candle range — to be A/B tested in backtests rather than gating trades.
The #1 prohibition Never counter-trend after a Level-1 move — not even on a picture-perfect reversal candle at the 200 EMA. "We do not counter-trend trade after a Level-1 rise (or drop)." That single 200-EMA reversal candle right after Leg 1 is the classic trap.

e. M and W patterns — her reversal signature

An M (double top) marks a top; a W (double bottom) marks a bottom. But she only trusts one that appears after a completed 3-leg move, sitting right at the HOW/LOW or HOD/LOD. A random M/W in the middle of nowhere is ignored.

Her "perfect M" is where the market maker shows up three times:

  1. An SVC at the first peak (the exhaustion candle above).
  2. Three trap candles — three same-colour candles pushing back into the level on the timeframe one step down (15m→5m), the third ending in a failed wick-spike into the SVC box. Retail sees a "bull flag"; it's bait.
  3. A 50-EMA break with volume, then a clean retest of the 50 (wicks touch it, bodies don't cross back).

The second peak is a lower high (for an M) — it must not re-print the first peak's extreme. Your entry cue is the first opposite-colour candle after the three trap candles. And the wicks must "snatch away" fast — a slow, lazy pullback is just profit-taking, not an M/W.

The multi-session M/W — her #1 weekly entry

The strongest version: peak 1 forms in one session, peak 2 in a later session or the next day and falls short on purpose. Her favourite is peak 1 in Asia, peak 2 at the UK open right at the HOW/LOD — she says this "almost guarantees" a 3-leg drop. It usually shows up when the 50 and 200 EMAs have widened apart. Entry is on the close of a reversal candle (hammer / inverted hammer, or "other reversal candle") at peak 2; the stop goes below the first spike's wick low / the LOD (mirror above for an M).

Igor ruling · 11 Jul 2026Peak-2 shortfall is soft. 25–35% short of the internal leg is the extreme edge; peak 2 only needs to land "in the vicinity" of peak 1 or it isn't an M/W. No hard threshold — calibrate it against Igor's 36 labelled weeks.

Final Damage (FDM / FDW) — the overshoot variant

Same M/W, but the second peak pokes just beyond the first (a Final-Damage W's second low dips below the first low; an FD M's second high pips above the first) and then closes back inside as a 15-minute hammer / inverted hammer. The overshoot is a wick, up to roughly 25% of the pattern's height. It has a higher failure rate but a better reward when it works. This is Setup 2.

f. Levels 1, 2, 3 — counting the legs

A trend runs in three legs, and she counts them from the last peak formation. Each leg is one "level":

  • Level 1 — breaks the 50 EMA with a volume vector, runs to the 200 EMA. (Roughly, this leg liquidates the ~100× leveraged traders.)
  • Level 2 — after a board-meeting pause that holds the 50, runs to the 800 EMA or the next unrecovered vector (~50× traders).
  • Level 3 — the final push, ending in an SVC. This is where the reversal is issued (~25× traders).
Retail patterns work at Level 1–2 and FAIL at Level 3 No breakout or chart-pattern trades at Level 3 — that's exactly where the market maker reverses. Once you've counted three legs, you're hunting the opposite M/W, not chasing the trend.

g. Board meetings — the pauses between legs

A "board meeting" = a consolidation where the market maker decides the next move.

Between each leg, price goes sideways (or retraces) while the EMAs flatten and bunch. Two flavours: a retracement board meeting (price pulls back — she maps it with a Fibonacci retracement, see Risk) and a sideways one (flat chop). Inside one, the fake-out wicks come early (first third) and the real stop-hunt comes late (near the end), right before the genuine breakout. While you're still learning, she says take partial profit at every board meeting and move your stop rather than holding blindly through it.

Igor ruling · 11 Jul 2026A board meeting is just a channel — her fancy name for a sideways channel. Usually sideways, it can slope up or down and can retrace back to the breaking PVSRA candle. There are no special boundary-drawing rules to learn.

h. Liquidity maps (targets only — we use free substitutes)

Two paid tools show where forced-liquidation orders sit, so she knows what the market maker is aiming at. Hyblock estimates liquidation levels (where over-leveraged traders get force-closed); TradingLite is a heatmap of resting limit orders (big "walls"). When a TradingLite wall lines up with a Hyblock pool she calls it "marrying" — a strong magnet. Crucial caveat: these are targets, never entry signals ("very dangerous" to trade off directly). If she's already long, she takes profit just in front of a pool. Note she charts OKX but her liquidity data is Binance — she accepts the mismatch, and so can you.

Igor ruling · 11 Jul 2026We subscribe to neither. TradingLite went out of business (useful but buggy); Hyblock is too expensive. Every liquidity/OI feature in this system uses free Coinalyze/Coinglass substitutes — treat the paid heatmaps below as the concept, not a required data feed.

Part 3

The 7 Setups

Listed in the order the course teaches them — roughly most-mechanical first — not the order to trade or backtest them. Your flagship and first backtest priority is the Weekly Setup / FMWB fade (S3); the Brinks trade leads the list only because it's the most clockwork, but it fires rarely, so trade and test it last (see the note on its card). Each one is: what it is, when it happens, the exact rules, where the stop and target go, a picture, and (where relevant) what the course leaves unanswered.

S1 · Most mechanical (rare)
The Brinks Trade

What it is: the most mechanical trade — an M/W that completes at a session changeover, at one of exactly two times a day.

When it happens: only on the 15-minute chart, and only the candle that closes at 3:45am or 9:45am New York (i.e. the 3:30–3:45 and 9:30–9:45 candles). Precondition: price is at the HOD/LOD, broke out of the Asia range near Asia's close, then retraced back into the changeover. The lead-in is ~3 fast bursts, with the second M/W peak forming 30–90 minutes after the first.

Igor's note — priority Rare but big — not the first setup to trade or backtest. Backtest (Jul 2026, 7 years of BTC): no edge as written — 50 trades, 36% win rate, lost money at every tested setting; only the long side in the UK window came close to breakeven. The weekly setup is the flagship.

The exact rules

  1. Wait for the 3:45 or 9:45 NY candle. It must close as an inverted hammer (for a short / M) or a hammer (for a long / W) — or as "railroad tracks" (two opposite candles of equal size).
  2. This must be the second peak of the M/W, at the HOD/LOD, after an Asia-range breakout-and-retrace.
  3. Enter at the open of the very next 15-minute candle (a market order).
  4. Then trail your stop behind each new consolidation zone as it moves your way.
Stop
The red zone drawn above both peaks of the M (mirror: below both lows of the W).
Target 1
The green zone at the origin (base) of the prior impulse vector candle — "recovery of the market-maker candle."
Time stop
If you're not in decent profit within 2 hours, close it. And never on weekends.
What we still don't know On the W (long) side she only gestures "under here" for the stop — first-peak low vs the hammer's low is never nailed down. And what precisely counts as "the market-maker candle" for the target is shown by gesture, not defined.

S2 · Priority 2
Final Damage M / W

What it is: the crispest single trigger — an M/W whose second peak overshoots the first, then snaps back.

When it happens: at the LOD/LOW (for a Final-Damage W) or HOD/HOW (FD M), after the legs are done.

The exact rules

  1. Price makes a second low below the first low (W), or a second high above the first high (M) — an overshoot, mostly a wick, up to ~25% of the pattern's height.
  2. That candle must close back inside as a 15-minute hammer (W) or inverted hammer (M) — body closing above the first low / below the first high.
  3. Enter on the close of that hammer.
Stop
Beyond the extreme peak of the formation — past the overshooting second peak (lowest low of an FD-W / highest high of an FD-M).
Target
Same as the weekly setup: ride the legs to the Level-1 vector and beyond.
Note
Higher failure rate than a plain M/W ("market structure hasn't changed"), but better reward. Journal it before sizing up.
Igor ruling · 11 Jul 2026 — resolved The Final-Damage stop goes beyond the extreme peak of the formation — i.e. past the overshooting second peak (the lowest low of an FD-W / the highest high of an FD-M). Igor: "of course it's going to be lower than the lowest, or higher than the highest, peak of that formation." The "Stop Loss" label on her slide belongs to the trapped victim's trade, not this entry — "stop at the first peak" is impossible here because the second peak is the new extreme.

S3 · Priority 3
The Weekly Setup / FMWB Fade — the flagship

What it is: fade the Monday fake-out, then ride the real 3-leg trend. This is the core money-maker and the one your 40 backtested weeks are built around.

When it happens: the fake move fires around 5pm NY Sunday (can slip into Monday's Asia/UK/US). Then the real trend develops through mid-week.

The exact rules

  1. Draw the weekend box from the first candle after Friday's US close — sized to candle-close clusters, not wick tips.
  2. Wait for the Sun/Mon spike-out of that box (the FMWB) and an M / W / Final-Damage forming — ideally the multi-session version.
  3. On the 1-hour chart, count the levels from the last peak formation; drop to 15-minute to time the entry. (Three levels on 15m can equal one level on 1H — don't exit early.)
  4. Enter on a valid retest after Level 1 (see the retest menu below), with the stop beyond the second peak.
  5. Take partials at each board meeting; final exit at Level 3 + SVC, or by the end of Friday UK.

The retest menu (which pullback to enter on)

Level 1 must first break the 50 EMA with a vector candle (red or green PVSRA, the 200% class). After that, a re-entry is valid when 2 of these 4 line up (ideally more):

  • a 50-EMA retest,
  • a return to the Level-1 vector,
  • a higher-low (uptrend) / lower-high (downtrend) structure,
  • a heatmap / liquidation level.
Igor ruling · 11 Jul 2026Precondition + 2-of-4. The 50 EMA must be broken by a vector candle (red/green PVSRA = 200% class) first; then take the retest when 2 of the 4 conditions above are present. We run no Hyblock/TradingLite, so ④ (heatmap/liq) comes from free Coinalyze/Coinglass liquidation data — or the rule falls back to 2-of-3 when liq data is unavailable.
Stop
Beyond the second peak of the M/W. Invalidated only by a candle closing back past the second peak — a weak, no-volume push through Level 1 is not an exit.
Target 1
The origin of the Level-1 vector (the good-reward target). "Initial target only to Level 1 — anything after is cream on top."
Refund zone
Cut a second-peak entry back to about break-even if a candle closes through the second-peak wick.

S4 · Priority 4
Mid-Week Reversal (MWR)

What it is: from Wednesday, catch the week's trend flipping over — usually on news.

When it happens: Wed/Thu, lined up with a Forex-Factory news event (CPI, FOMC, Core PCE). Your MWR backtest tab shows these cluster Tuesday/Wednesday, mostly in the US session.

The exact rules

  1. The week's trend has run its three legs into the HOW/LOW.
  2. Find the SVC at Level 3, then an M/W forming at the extreme (entry mechanics are the same as the weekly setup — enter the second peak, stop above the trap wick band).
  3. If a vertical move plus an extended board meeting means "the damage is done," the three levels may complete on the 15-minute chart — take profit before the news, don't hold through it.
Stop
Above the trap-wick band of the M (below for a W), same as S3.
Target
Printed example: 4.6 : 1 if you close in the UK session, 6 : 1 if you hold to the LOW.
Skip-it rule
If the news forces a wide stop and wrecks the reward, skip the trade. Never hold through scheduled news with 3 levels already counted.

S5 · Priority 5
3 Hits to a Level

What it is: when price fails at the same level three times, the reversal is near.

When it happens: at Level 3, at the HOW/LOW, read on ~15-minute.

The exact rules

  1. Count "hits" on the level: a hit = an upper wick tagging (or hair-piercing) the level with no candle closing beyond it.
  2. Hits must span at least 2 sessions (max ~2 per session) — three in one session doesn't count.
  3. 3 failed hits → reversal imminent. A 4th hit → continuation (the level will break instead).
  4. Tiebreaker after hit 3: draw a trendline under the rising lows. Another higher low → it breaks up (stand down); a clear lower high, then that trendline breaks → down (short it).
What we still don't know She never states the exact entry, stop and target for the 3-hits trade on its own — they're almost certainly the weekly setup's (enter the reversal candle, stop beyond the level, target the vector), but that's inference.

S6 · Priority 6
Backflip Swing

What it is: not a new signal — a way to stay in a position continuously by flipping long↔short at each reversal. It sits on top of S3–S5.

The idea (never be flat): ride the three rises long → at the Level-3 SVC, bank partial profit and open a 25% "starter" short (still hedged by the open long) → at the second M peak, add the rest of the short and close the long → repeat on the way down. Add more on 3-hits.

Position 1
25% of the initial trade's position size Igor ruling, entered at the SVC candle close, stop under its wick.
Position 2
75%, added at the second-peak reversal candle.
Exit
At "3 drops + SVC" on the other side. A completed flip prints around 4.8 : 1.
Igor ruling · 11 Jul 2026 — corrects the slide The starter is 25% of the initial trade's position size ($10,000 initial → $2,500 opposite short), not 25% of a 1% risk unit. Alternative Igor allows: 33% three times. This supersedes the slide's "25% of your 1% total risk." See Staggering.

She flags a prerequisite: only build positions like this once you have ~6 months / 100 trades with a solid win rate and reward ratio. Until then, one clean entry per setup.

S7 · Overlay
Open-Interest Filter

What it is: a free on/off filter that tells you whether to expect a stop-hunt before the breakout.

Open Interest (OI) = how many leveraged contracts are currently open. Reading it alongside a board meeting tells you how the breakout will behave:

  • Board meeting + OI rising → breakout is coming, no stop-hunt needed — you may even enter inside the consolidation.
  • Board meeting + OI flat/falling → expect a stop-hunt first; wait, then enter on the hunt.
  • Retrace + OI rising → traders are being trapped in an M/W before continuation → re-enter at the prior vector.
  • OI ramping into news → the news delivers Level 3, then watch for the mid-week reversal.

Not a setup
The Friday Weekend Trap — an exit rule

On the weekly timeframe the Friday trap is a take-profit signal only, never an entry. Sequence (NY time): a false move at the UK open → a few hours counter-trend → often a consolidation around 8am → a repeat of the false move near the UK close, revisiting the same extreme. If your three levels are done (or even just two), take profit and be flat by the end of Friday UK. Never carry a majors trade over the weekend.

Part 4

Risk & Trade Management

The setups make money only if the sizing and stops are disciplined. These are her hard rules.

Size & reward

  • 1% of the account at risk per built position — not per order.
  • Minimum reward-to-risk 1.9 : 1. On screen she accepts a Target 2.77% / Stop 1.45% trade (= 1.91) and explicitly rejects a "1.4 : 1 RR — Skip It." Sub-2:1 = "we don't get out of bed for that."
  • Printed reward examples across her worked weeks run 3.5 : 1 to 6 : 1 for single setups, up to 16–20 : 1 when all three legs are held.

Staggering into a position (25 / 75)

She rarely enters all at once. The standard split is 25% at the SVC (entered at candle close) and 75% at the second-peak reversal. A finer version scales 25 / 25 / 50 across Fibonacci retracement levels (38.2 / 50 / 61.8, plus her custom gold 65% line). The Fib is anchored 100% at the top of the most recent completed leg, 0% at its low, and the stop sits at the 100% Fib (above the market-maker candle zone).

Igor ruling · 11 Jul 2026 — corrects the slide Backflip starter size. The opposite "starter" position is 25% of the initial trade's position size, not 25% of a 1% risk unit — initial trade $10,000 → opposite short $2,500. (Alternative Igor allows: 33% three times.) This supersedes the slide reading of "25% of your 1% total risk."

The stop-move law

  • Move the stop to break-even only after the retracement has happened and Level 2 has started — not before.
  • Once moved, never park the stop below your initial target. If price stalls, it goes to exactly break-even, "nothing in between" (so a 1.9-reward trade never resolves as a scrappy 1:1 and corrupts your stats).
  • Every stop-move point is also a partial-profit point. Take some off at each board meeting.
  • Asia range > 2% → don't trade that day. Don't hold through scheduled news with 3 levels counted.

The clock: how long the pattern takes (the "durations doc")

She has a reference doc of minimum candle counts on the 15-minute chart, so you don't bail too early. A W formation takes roughly 11.5 hours; a full M-plus-W cycle about 23 hours. Trade the timeframe where the model is actually playing out — if it's on the 4-hour, watching only the 15-minute will make you exit rises too early and enter fake reversals the wrong way.

Part 5

Questions — Answered by Igor (11 Jul 2026)

These were the genuine blanks in the course. Igor answered all eight on 11 July 2026, and his rulings are final — they override every earlier interpretation and are now woven into the rule sections above (look for the gold Igor ruling tags). Each card keeps its "Where this comes from" block — the source video, timestamp, and what she actually said — for the record. Where Igor's answer corrects a slide reading (Q7), it's flagged explicitly.

1Retest validity — how many, out of which conditions?Answered by Igor Igor's ruling: Precondition — the 50 EMA must first be broken by a vector candle (red or green PVSRA, the 200% class). Then a retest entry is valid when 2 of these 4 line up: ① a 50-EMA retest, ② a return to the Level-1 vector, ③ higher-low (uptrend) / lower-high (downtrend) structure, ④ a heatmap / liquidation level. With no Hyblock/TradingLite, ④ comes from free Coinalyze/Coinglass liquidation data — or the rule runs as 2-of-3 when liq data is unavailable.
Where this comes fromSource: The Weekly Setup V2 [06:02–07:33].She lists four valid retests — 50-EMA retest, return to the Level-1 vector, higher-low/lower-high structure, and a heatmap/liquidation level — then says price should retrace to "a combination of at least 2 of the 3 conditions." The screen showed only a static "!!! DO NOT SHORT THIS !!!" 200-EMA image the whole time, so the "2 of 3 vs 4" slip can't be settled from the video.Jump to the rules this affects →
2SVC volume multiplier — how big must the spike be?Answered by Igor Igor's ruling: The size of the spike is subjective; the shape is fixed — a PVSRA high-volume candle (higher volume than usual) with a long wick (either side) and a body smaller than the wick. It's a "stopping volume candle" — price stops there and usually reverses. On timeframes under 1H, an engulfing candle also qualifies as the stopping signal.
Where this comes fromSource: v2-VSC and Volume [04:02] · v2 M and W's [17:37].She defines the SVC as "a small bodied candle with a large wick with a spike in volume at level three." Both worked examples were the 150% (blue) class, not 200% — an SVC only needs to be PVSRA-coloured with a volume bar that towers over its neighbours (eyeballed ~3–5×). No fixed multiple is ever stated.Jump to the rules this affects →
3Final-Damage stop placement.Answered by Igor Igor's ruling: The stop goes beyond the extreme peak of the formation — past the overshooting second peak (the lowest low of an FD-W / the highest high of an FD-M). In his words: "of course it's going to be lower than the lowest, or higher than the highest, peak of that formation."
Where this comes fromSource: Final Damage M and W [00:45–02:00].The FD stop is never spoken or drawn. The "Entry" and "Stop Loss" labels on her slide belong to the trapped victim's long (the trade that gets spiked) — the entry precedes the second low and the stop sits above the pattern. The usual "stop at the first peak" is impossible here because the second peak is the new extreme.Jump to the rules this affects →
4Sideways board-meeting boundaries.Answered by Igor Igor's ruling: A board meeting is just a channel — her fancy name for a sideways channel. Usually sideways, it can slope up or down and can retrace back to the breaking PVSRA candle. There are no special boundary-drawing rules.
Where this comes fromSource: v2-Board Meetings [01:50–04:10].A retracement board meeting is drawn with a Fib from the prior leg's swing high (100%) to its low (0%), including her custom gold 65% level. A sideways one is only ever shown as a horizontal range with the EMAs braided flat — she circles inducement wicks inside it but never draws its edges as an object.Jump to the rules this affects →
5Peak-2 shortfall on a multi-session M/W.Answered by Igor Igor's ruling (soft): 25–35% short of the internal leg is the extreme edge; peak 2 only needs to be "in the vicinity" of peak 1 or it isn't an M/W. No hard number — calibrate the threshold against his 36 labelled weeks.
Where this comes fromSource: M and W Multi Session V2 [03:02–04:32].Her one measured slide shows peak 2 recovering roughly two-thirds to three-quarters of the internal pullback — i.e. stopping ~25–35% short of the internal leg. In words she only says the second peak comes back "not as close"; no numeric tolerance is ever taught.Jump to the rules this affects →
6Do you currently have Hyblock and/or TradingLite subscriptions?Answered by Igor Igor's ruling: Neither. TradingLite went out of business (useful but buggy); Hyblock is too expensive. All liquidity/OI features use free Coinalyze/Coinglass substitutes.
Where this comes fromSource: v2-Heatmaps and Hyblock · v2-tradinglite · v2-Liquidation levels.Both are paid — Hyblock estimates liquidation pools (Advanced plan); TradingLite is the order-book heatmap. She uses them only as targets — "very dangerous" to trade the levels as direct reversals — and even front-runs them for take-profit (pool at $25,000 → TP at $24,950). Your answer decides whether we build around them or substitute free liquidation estimates.Jump to the rules this affects →
7Backflip starter short size.Answered by Igor Igor's ruling (corrects the slide): The starter is 25% of the initial trade's position size — initial trade $10,000 → opposite short $2,500 — not 25% of a 1% risk unit. Alternative he allows: 33% three times. This ruling supersedes the slide reading. See also the Backflip Swing card.
Where this comes fromSource: Annii's Swing Trading System ~[00:00–01:30] — the "W or M Staggered Entries" slide (4H, Binance).Her slide prints it verbatim: "Position 1, 25% of your 1% total risk" entered at the SVC ("SVC identified — enter at candle close"), and "Position 2, 75% of your 1% total risk" at the second-peak reversal candle. Igor's ruling supersedes this slide reading: the starter is 25% of the initial trade's position size ($10,000 initial → $2,500 opposite short), not 25% of a 1% risk unit.Jump to the rules this affects →
8EMA "fan-out" number.Answered by Igor Igor's ruling (judgment call): There is no number. Demote fan-out from a hard filter to an optional proxy — e.g. the EMA gap ≥ 2–3× the recent candle range — to be A/B tested in backtests.
Where this comes fromSource: v2-EMA's [08:04–09:10]."Fanning out" is shown as two giant momentum candles pulling the 10 EMA away from the stack, leaving a visible gap to the 50 (eyeballed ~2–3× a normal candle's range); "flattening" is all five EMAs braided into one flat horizontal band. She circles the gap but never states a number — it stays a judgment call.Jump to the rules this affects →

Part 6

Glossary — every term, one line

Market Maker (MM)
The big player who moves price and runs the weekly script; you trade the other side of its traps.
PVSRA
Free indicator that colours candles by volume (see the colour key in Part 2a).
Vector
A high-volume coloured candle — a big player's footprint.
Unrecovered vector
A vector price hasn't traded back through yet; acts as a magnet and target.
SVC
Stopping Volume Candle — small body, long wick, volume spike at Level 3; signals exhaustion.
SVC box
The SVC's full wick-to-wick range extended right; invalidated only by a close through its far edge.
EMA
Exponential moving average — a trend line weighted to recent price.
EMA stack
Her 10/20/50/200/800 EMAs on every timeframe.
Fan-out
EMAs spreading wide at Level 3 — looks like momentum, actually the trap.
M pattern
Double top; her short signal at the HOD/HOW after 3 legs.
W pattern
Double bottom; her long signal at the LOD/LOW after 3 legs.
Trap candles
Three same-colour candles luring retail before the reversal (retail calls it a flag).
Multi-session M/W
M/W whose two peaks fall in different sessions/days; her strongest weekly entry.
Final Damage (FDM/FDW)
M/W whose second peak overshoots the first then snaps back as a hammer.
Level 1 / 2 / 3
The three legs of a trend, counted from the last peak; patterns fail at Level 3.
Board meeting
A consolidation/pause between legs while the MM decides direction.
Damage is done
The final Level-3 stop-run is complete; no more legs owed.
HOW / LOW
High / Low Of the Week (since Sunday 5pm NY).
HOD / LOD
High / Low Of the Day (since 5pm NY).
IHOD / ILOD
The inner high/low, one step inside HOD/LOD.
Area of Interest (AOI)
The shaded band between an inner and outer level; a reaction zone.
Dead Zone
The quiet post-US session; the daily 5pm NY reset lives here.
Asia / UK / US session
Ranges / trends / reverses — the day's three active characters.
FMWB
"Fake move at week's beginning" — the Sun/Mon stop-hunt spike.
MWR
Mid-Week Reversal — the Wed/Thu trend flip, usually on news.
Weekend box
Box drawn from Friday's US close to close-clusters; the fake move spikes out of it.
Weekend trap
Friday's false move at UK open/close — a take-profit cue, never an entry.
Brinks
The 3:45am / 9:45am NY session-changeover M/W trade (Setup 1).
Backflip
Continuously flipping long↔short at each reversal so you're never flat.
3 hits to a level
Three failed tests of a level = reversal near; a 4th = it breaks.
Open Interest (OI)
Number of leveraged contracts open; used to predict stop-hunts.
Stop hunt
A spike engineered to trigger stops before the real move.
Liquidation level
Price where over-leveraged traders get force-closed; a target (Hyblock).
Heatmap
Map of resting limit-order "walls" (TradingLite).
Marrying
A TradingLite wall coinciding with a Hyblock pool — a strong magnet.
Hyblock
Paid liquidation-level estimator (Binance data); not subscribed — free Coinalyze/Coinglass substitute.
TradingLite
Paid order-book heatmap; now defunct — free Coinalyze/Coinglass substitute.
Hammer / Inverted hammer
Small-body, long-wick reversal candles (bottom / top respectively).
Railroad tracks
Two opposite, equal-size candles marking a reversal.
Fixed Range Volume Profile
Tool showing volume by price; a low-volume "crevice" confirms a target.
R:R (reward-to-risk)
Potential profit ÷ potential loss; her minimum is 1.9.
Staggering
Entering a position in tranches (25/75 or 25/25/50).
Break-even
Moving the stop to your entry so the trade can't lose.
Refund zone
The exit that cuts a second-peak entry back to break-even if the wick is lost.
Trapped traders
Retail positioned wrong by the MM's fake move — the fuel for the reversal.
Built for Igor from the Trading TTC course synthesis (the source notes + 24 video digests). Every rule traces to her videos or your backtest notes; unresolved items are flagged, not invented. · Click any screenshot to open it full-size.
